name: The Goat's Path Climb
name_original: Subida Carretera de las Cabras — ida y vuelta por el Parque Natural Sierra de Tejada
description: This difficult 37.8-mile road cycling route, the Subida Carretera de las Cabras, offers challenging climbs and stunning views in Sierra de T
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Itrabo offer a blend of challenging mountainous terrain and scenic coastal paths in the province of Granada, Spain. The region is characterized by significant elevation changes, verdant river valleys, and terraced cultivation featuring olive groves, vineyards, and tropical fruit orchards. Cyclists can experience diverse landscapes, from pine and eucalyptus forests to sweeping views of the Mediterranean Sea and the Granada Mountains. This geographical position provides varied backdrops for road cycling. Best road cycling routes around Itrabo The most popular road cycling route is Nerja, Frigiliana, and Torrox Loop, a 55.4 miles (89.2 km) trail that takes 4 hours 54 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gain and views of the coast and inland villages. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is The Goat's Path Climb, a difficult 37.8 miles (60.8 km) path. This route includes demanding ascents and panoramic views of the surrounding mountains. Local road cyclists also love the Carretera de la Cabra – Recta de la Cabra loop from Almuñécar, a 76.9 miles (123.8 km) trail leading through varied terrain and mountain passes, often completed in about 6 hours 27 minutes. Road cycling around Itrabo is defined by mountainous ascents, coastal paths, and agricultural landscapes. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to demanding climbs. The routes in Itrabo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.2 stars from over 130 reviews. More than 8,000 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Itrabo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Itrabo?
A: Road cycling around Itrabo offers a diverse mix of challenging mountainous terrain, scenic coastal paths, and agricultural landscapes. You'll encounter significant elevation changes, verdant river valleys, and terraced cultivation featuring centenary olive groves, vineyards, and tropical fruit orchards. Routes often wind through areas with pine and eucalyptus trees, providing varied backdrops and stunning views of both the Granada Mountains and the Mediterranean Sea.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners or those looking for an easier ride?
A: Yes, while Itrabo is known for its challenging climbs, there are options for all ability levels. Out of over 50 road cycling routes in the area, &lt;em&gt;7 are classified as easy&lt;/em&gt;, offering a more relaxed experience while still allowing you to enjoy the region's beauty. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39548529" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Salobreña – Salobreña Castle loop from Velilla-Taramay&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate option that provides coastal views without extreme difficulty.

Q: What are some of the most scenic viewpoints or landmarks to look out for on a road cycling tour?
A: Many routes offer stunning 360-degree views of the Granada Mountains and the Mediterranean coast. You can also explore historical and natural landmarks nearby. Consider visiting the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1090203" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de Salobreña&lt;/a&gt; for historical insights or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/87627" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Río Verde Waterfalls, Barranco de las Chortales&lt;/a&gt; for natural beauty. The region's terraced fields of tropical fruits and olive groves also provide picturesque scenery.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Itrabo?
A: The road cycling routes around Itrabo are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of &lt;strong&gt;4.2 stars from over 130 reviews&lt;/strong&gt;. Cyclists frequently praise the blend of challenging ascents and rewarding panoramic views of the coast and mountains. The diverse landscapes, from agricultural valleys to pine forests, are also a highlight, offering a constantly changing and visually appealing experience.

Q: Can I find circular road cycling routes in the Itrabo area?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Itrabo are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end your ride in the same location. An example of a challenging circular route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/3954308" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Carretera de la Cabra – Recta de la Cabra loop from Almuñécar&lt;/a&gt;, which covers a significant distance through varied terrain and mountain passes.

Q: Are there any routes that pass by cafes or places to stop for refreshments?
A: While specific cafes on every route are not detailed, the region's charming white villages, like Itrabo itself, offer local food and tapas. Many routes connect coastal towns and villages where you can find establishments for refreshments. Planning your route to pass through these settlements will allow for convenient stops.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Itrabo?
A: Itrabo offers good cycling conditions for much of the year. The varied elevation means you can find suitable temperatures. While the high ground can experience snow in winter, lower elevations maintain tropical landscapes. Spring and autumn generally provide pleasant temperatures for cycling, avoiding the peak heat of summer and the cooler, potentially snowy conditions at higher altitudes in winter.

Q: Are there any family-friendly road cycling options around Itrabo?
A: While Itrabo is known for its challenging terrain, there are 7 easy-rated routes that could be more suitable for families or those seeking a less strenuous ride. These routes typically feature less elevation gain and cover shorter distances, allowing for a more relaxed pace. Always check the route details for specific difficulty and elevation profiles to ensure it matches your family's capabilities.

Q: What are the options for parking when road cycling in Itrabo?
A: Itrabo and the surrounding towns and villages offer various parking options. When planning your ride, consider starting points in larger towns like Almuñécar or Salobreña, which typically have more designated parking areas. Always check local signage for parking regulations and availability.

Q: Can I reach the road cycling routes around Itrabo using public transport with my bicycle?
A: Public transport options in the region, particularly buses, may allow bicycles, though policies can vary. It's advisable to check with local bus operators in advance regarding their specific regulations for transporting bicycles, especially during peak times or on smaller routes. Starting your ride from a coastal town like Motril or Almuñécar, which have better transport links, might be more convenient.

Q: How long do road cycling routes typically take around Itrabo?
A: The duration of road cycling routes around Itrabo varies significantly based on distance, elevation, and your cycling pace. For example, the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e981410528/nerja-frigiliana-and-torrox-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Nerja, Frigiliana, and Torrox Loop&lt;/a&gt;, at 89.2 km, typically takes around 4 hours and 54 minutes. Shorter, easier routes will naturally take less time, while longer, more demanding ones like the 123.8 km &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/3954308" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Carretera de la Cabra – Recta de la Cabra loop from Almuñécar&lt;/a&gt; can take over 6 hours.

Q: Are there any waterfalls or natural water features accessible from the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, the region boasts beautiful natural water features. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/87627" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Río Verde Waterfalls, Barranco de las Chortales&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/87629" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Petrified Trees Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; are notable attractions nearby. While not directly on every road cycling route, they can be incorporated into your trip planning for a scenic detour or a post-ride exploration.
tips: Viewpoint that appears on the Carretera de la Cabra from where you can observe the views of the Sierra de Cázulas and the Valle de Otívar.
Spectacular switchbacks, tunnels, and vistas on this sparsely traveled mountain road
Road that goes in the direction of the town of Ítrabo and from where you can see an immense view of the valley that goes down to Molvízar and Motril.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginner road cyclists?
faqs.answer: No, this route is generally considered a difficult road ride, requiring very good fitness. It involves significant elevation gain and is classified as a "first-category climb," comparable to many famous Tour de France cols. It's best suited for experienced road cyclists.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Carretera de la Cabra route?
faqs.answer: The route features a significant climb from the coast inland, with numerous spectacular switchbacks and tunnels. While much of the surface is well-paved, some sections can have degrading tarmac with potholes and cracks, especially further inland. The landscape transitions from lush avocado plantations to drier, more mountainous, alpine-like terrain higher up.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter several notable spots, including the Viewpoint of La Cabra Montés, the Carretera de la Cabra Tunnel, and the View of the Valley from the Ítrabo Road. The route also offers a scenic Descent to Otívar through Sierra de Tejeda, Almijara y Alhama Natural Park.

faqs.question: When is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is typically during the spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November) months. During these periods, the weather is generally milder and more pleasant for cycling, avoiding the intense heat of summer and potential colder, wetter conditions of winter.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas aren't detailed, many cyclists begin this route from coastal towns like Almuñécar, where you can find public parking. Alternatively, you might find parking in villages like Jete or Otívar along the route, depending on your preferred starting point for the loop.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for cycling in the Parque Natural Sierra de Tejada?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for cycling on public roads within the Parque Natural Sierra de Tejada, Almijara y Alhama. However, always respect local regulations and private property signage.

faqs.question: Are there places to refill water or get refreshments along the way?
faqs.answer: The route passes through charming villages like Jete and Otívar where you can find services. However, in the higher, more remote sections, services can be scarce. It's highly recommended to carry enough water and snacks, especially for the challenging climbs.

faqs.question: Is the Subida Carretera de las Cabras route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This is a road cycling route, so while dogs are generally allowed in the natural park, bringing a dog along for a road bike ride of this difficulty and length is not practical or safe. It's not considered a dog-friendly activity in the traditional sense.

faqs.question: What makes the Carretera de la Cabra route unique?
faqs.answer: This route is unique for its historical significance as an ancient mule trade route, its stunning and continuously changing vistas from coast to mountains, and its reputation as a challenging and renowned climb for road cyclists. It also features numerous hairpin turns and tunnels, and often has low traffic, especially on weekdays.

faqs.question: What type of bike is best suited for this route?
faqs.answer: This route is specifically designed for racebikes (road bikes). While the main road is paved, some sections can have degrading tarmac. If you plan to venture off the main paved road onto variations that might include gravel sections, a suitable gravel bike would be necessary, but for the described round trip, a road bike is ideal.

faqs.question: How busy is the road cycling route?
faqs.answer: Compared to other routes in the region, the Carretera de la Cabra is often sparsely traveled, especially on weekdays. This can make for a more peaceful and enjoyable cycling experience, allowing you to fully appreciate the scenery without heavy traffic.
